Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered. I tried to put in around 19 hours a week, but it wasn't like I had a set schedule or anything. I'd usually find time between things, grabbing my notes or laptop whenever I could. Being a kinesthetic learner, I needed to do stuff with my hands, write things out, practice problems physically. I'd spread my materials out on my bed or floor and just work through things until I felt like I understood them.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due. Some weeks I'd be pretty consistent, maybe studying a bit each day, but when deadlines hit I'd cram way more hours in. With 10 online courses going on, it was hard to keep everything straight. I'd often have to prioritize whichever class had something due soonest, so my routine shifted constantly based on that pressure.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline coming up or needing to check if there was new content posted. Sometimes I'd get a notification on my phone and that would remind me to log in. I logged in pretty regularly, like 15 times or so, mostly because I was trying to stay on top of things even when motivation was low. The discussion posts also pulled me in since I knew participation mattered.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks it became kind of automatic to check the LMS in the morning or before bed. I'd just open it up like checking social media, scrolling through announcements and seeing what was new. The mindfulness exercises they had us do actually helped me create that habit because I'd log in for those and then end up checking other stuff while I was there.
Q5: The video content was really useful for me, I watched over an hour's worth pretty regularly. Videos helped because I could pause them and actually try things out myself, which fits how I learn best. The discussion boards were also good because interacting with other students helped concepts stick better than just reading alone. When I could see how other people approached problems, it clicked more for me.
Q6: I'll be honest, I often skipped over the heavy reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. With limited resources at home and not using any edu-tech tools, downloading and reading long documents felt like a hassle. I'd skim them at best or just rely on the videos to cover the same content. Some optional quizzes I also ignored because they didn't count toward my grade.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to assignments or exams. If I could do something hands-on or interactive, that made it way more valuable to me. I also cared more when I knew it would help my grade since, honestly, with my exam scores being rough, I needed every point I could get. Activities that felt like busywork without clear purpose got pushed aside.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different, like they were extras I could skip if I was overwhelmed. Required ones had this weight to them, this pressure that I had to complete them no matter what. With optional stuff, I'd tell myself I'd get to it later but often never did. The stress of keeping up with required work made optional activities feel like luxuries I couldn't afford.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides or print out notes when I could get to a printer, then study from those physical copies. Writing things out by hand, making my own diagrams, working through practice problems on paper - that's how I actually learned. The LMS wouldn't show any of that work, but it was where most of my real studying happened.
Q10: There were tons of times I was genuinely studying but the LMS had no clue. I'd watch a video once, take notes, then spend hours reviewing those notes offline. Or I'd work through problems with a classmate in person, talking through concepts together. My peer interactions showed as 9 in the system, but we'd meet up outside of class too and that collaboration really helped me understand things.
Q11: Yeah, definitely. I'd download a PDF or save lecture slides, then never open them in the LMS again. I'd study from those saved files for days, going over them repeatedly. The system would show I accessed something once, but in reality I was reviewing that material multiple times offline. It was just easier than logging in every single time, especially when my internet wasn't always reliable.
Q12: There were definitely periods where engaging got really hard. When stress was high and my motivation was dragging, I'd feel this heaviness about even opening my laptop. Around midterms especially, with 10 courses demanding attention, I felt spread too thin. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me push through some of those moments, but it was still a struggle to stay focused.
Q13: Workload was a huge factor. Having so many online courses meant I was constantly juggling priorities. Fatigue hit hard sometimes, and when I was tired, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those sections because I didn't know where to even start. My low resources meant I couldn't always access help when I needed it.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd open videos or readings and my mind would just wander. I'd technically be on the LMS, time would pass, but nothing was sinking in. Sometimes I'd watch a whole video and realize I couldn't remember a single thing from it. Those moments were frustrating because I was putting in the time but not getting anything out of it.
Q15: I definitely postponed things, especially assignments that seemed complicated or overwhelming. Even knowing they were important, I'd put them off because starting felt impossible. My assignment completion rate wasn't great because of this. Sometimes the anxiety of not understanding something made me avoid it entirely, which I know made things worse in the long run.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my LMS usage. I'd be relatively quiet on the platform until something was due, then suddenly I'd be logging in constantly, downloading materials, posting discussions. The urgency of a deadline was often the only thing that could overcome my low motivation. Without that external pressure, I'd drift and lose focus on the course.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did affect how I engaged. Positive feedback made me want to keep going, while negative feedback sometimes discouraged me and made me avoid that area of the course. The problem was feedback didn't always come quickly, so I'd move on to other things before knowing if I was on the right track. Faster feedback would have helped me adjust my studying sooner.
Q18: The course structure had a big impact. When things were clearly organized and I knew what was expected, I participated more consistently. But when modules felt disjointed or requirements were unclear, I'd get confused and disengage. Having 10 courses meant I gravitated toward the ones with clearer structures because I didn't have mental energy to figure out confusing setups.
Q19: The video content being available was definitely helpful since that matched my learning style better than walls of text. The discussion boards being active made engagement easier too. What made it harder was when there were too many small tasks that felt disconnected from each other. Also, not having built-in interactive or hands-on elements meant I had to create those experiences myself offline.
Q20: I think that happens when students are trying to figure things out but struggling to produce finished work. You might be watching videos, reading discussions, trying to understand concepts, but when it comes to actually completing assignments, something breaks down. For me, understanding something and being able to demonstrate it on an exam or assignment were two different things. The LMS activity shows effort, just not successful output.
Q21: That kind of reflects my situation in reverse, actually. I had decent LMS activity but my exam scores were rough. But I can see how someone who studies efficiently offline might not need to be on the platform much. If you have good notes, textbooks, or study groups, you might learn everything you need without logging in constantly. The LMS isn't the only place learning happens.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, how often you log in, what you click on. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually understanding material or just going through the motions. They also miss all the offline work, the conversations with classmates, the handwritten notes, the thinking you do away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just showing up or clicking through content. For me, engagement is when I'm actively working with material, asking questions, trying to connect ideas. It's a mental and emotional investment, not just physical presence on a platform.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with motivation or how the mindfulness intervention actually helped my mood improve. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying offline with handwritten notes spread everywhere. It also can't show that despite failing, I was genuinely trying and engaged in my own way. The numbers don't tell the whole story of someone's effort or circumstances.
